title: Scene Size-Up in Trauma Care: A Comprehensive Guide for Emergency Providers
learningObjectives: Discuss the steps of the scene size-up.
List the two basic mechanisms of motion injury.
Identify the three collisions associated with a motor-vehicle collision (MVC) and relate potential patient injuries to deformity of the vehicle, interior structures, and body structures.
Name the five common forms of MVCs.
Describe potential injuries from proper and improper use of seat restraints, headrests, and air bags in a head-on collision.
Describe potential injuries from rear-end collisions.
Describe the three assessment criteria for falls and relate them to anticipated injuries.
Identify the two most common forms of penetrating injuries and discuss associated mechanisms and extent of injuries.
Relate five injury mechanisms involved in blast injuries and how they relate to scene size-up and patient assessment.

summary.title: Scene Size-Up Overview
summary.content: Scene size-up is the first step in the ITLS Primary survey. It involves taking standard precautions, evaluating the scene for dangers, determining the total number of patients, identifying essential equipment needed, and recognizing mechanisms of injuries. This process helps prevent exposure to blood and other potentially infectious materials and ensures patient safety.

summary.title: Standard Precautions
summary.content: Emergency care providers must take standard precautions such as wearing gloves, using a face shield in many situations, and sometimes protective gowns or chemical suits. These measures are crucial for preventing exposure to blood and other potentially infectious materials (OPIM).

summary.title: Scene Safety
summary.content: Before approaching the patient, assess the scene for potential dangers such as fire, toxic substances, unstable structures, low oxygen levels, or hazardous chemicals. Determine a safe place to park the ambulance and decide if it is safe to approach the patient.

summary.title: Total Number of Patients
summary.content: Determine the total number of patients present at the scene. If more than one patient is involved, call for additional resources and establish medical command or multiple-casualty incident (MCI) protocols. Carefully evaluate the scene to ensure all patients are accounted for.

summary.title: Essential Equipment
summary.content: Carry essential equipment such as personal protective equipment, patient transport devices, oxygen and airway equipment, trauma boxes, and special extrication tools if needed. Ensure that you have enough resources to handle the number of patients present.

summary.title: Mechanism of Injury (MOI)
summary.content: Assess the mechanism of injury by considering factors such as direction and speed of impact, patient kinetics, physical size, and signs of energy release. This information helps predict potential injuries and guide further assessment.

clinicalPearls: Always wear personal protective equipment (PPE) to prevent exposure to blood and other potentially infectious materials.
Be aware that a high-energy event may result in severe injuries even if the patient initially appears stable.
Use the three-collision concept when assessing MVCs: machine collision, body collision, and organ collision.
redFlags: Do not enter hazardous areas such as those with potential for fire or toxic chemicals without proper protective equipment.
Be cautious of secondary collisions involving objects inside a vehicle that may become projectiles during an accident.
Recognize the signs of a high-energy event, which can lead to severe injuries even if vital signs are initially normal.
commonMistakes: Failing to perform a scene size-up before approaching the patient, which can result in exposure to hazardous materials or failure to anticipate serious injuries.
Overlooking the need for additional equipment or personnel when more patients than expected are present.
Not considering the mechanism of injury thoroughly, leading to missed or overlooked critical injuries.
memoryAids: Remember that standard precautions include wearing gloves and a face shield in most situations.
Use the mnemonic 'SOS' (Scene, Safety, Patients) to recall the initial steps of scene size-up.
For MVCs, remember the three collisions: machine collision, body collision, and organ collision.

decisionTrees.title: Decision Tree for Scene Size-Up
decisionTrees.content:
1. Assess the scene for safety.
2. Determine if standard precautions are needed (gloves, face shield).
3. Identify potential hazards such as fire or toxic chemicals.
4. Count the number of patients and determine additional resources needed.
5. Evaluate the mechanism of injury to predict potential injuries.

references: Almogy, G., Y. Mintz, G. Zamir, T. Bdolah-Abram, R. Elazary, L. Dotan, M. Faruga, and A.I. Rivkind. “Suicide Bombing Attacks: Can External Signs Predict Internal Injuries?” Annals of Surgery 243, no. 4 (April 2006): 541–46.
Alson, R.L., and W. B. Patterson. Access: First on Scene—Rapid Vehicle Entry Provider Manual, 2nd ed. Downers Grove, IL: ITLS, 2006.
American Academy of Pediatrics, Committee on Injury and Poison Prevention. “Personal Watercraft Use by Children and Adolescents.” Pediatrics 105, no. 2 (February 1, 2000): 452–53.
